refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal scope and measurement context. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured over a defined period, and that the threshold is customer-specified rather than system-imposed. This remains solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, network architecture, or failover mechanisms—only the outcome requirement. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days). Calculate availability as (total_time - downtime) / total_time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Document any link outages, root causes, and recovery times. Acceptance: measured availability ≥ target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative pathways before availability threshold is breached.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth and latency management with dynamic protocol selection based on measured link conditions to sustain availability within acceptable performance bounds.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) + link quality monitoring (DP2) + autonomous fallback (DP3) as an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback mode, coordinated through unified state management and switchover logic.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP2 are tightly coupled: failover logic must consume quality metrics from monitoring to make switching decisions—this is intentional and necessary.
DP3 (autonomous fallback) introduces a secondary control domain that must synchronize state with remote operator upon link restoration—requires explicit handoff protocol to avoid command conflicts.
Coupling between pathway redundancy (DP1) and adaptive management (DP4) is moderate: different pathways may have different latency/bandwidth profiles, requiring DP4 to adapt per pathway.
State management across all three sub-DPs is a potential single point of failure if not carefully architected—recommend explicit state reconciliation protocol.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is a composite design that intentionally couples three sub-solutions to achieve the availability target. The couplings are justified by the need for coordinated failover and state consistency. Decoupling further (e.g., removing monitoring from failover logic) would degrade availability assurance. The design is acceptable if state synchronization and handoff protocols are explicitly specified in downstream IM layer.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: At least two independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP links). Risk: If only one pathway exists, DP1 (redundancy) cannot be realized; recommend constraint clarification.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable predictive detection (DP2). Risk: If measurement overhead or latency is high, predictive switching may fail; recommend measurement architecture specification.

ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous fallback mode (DP3) is operationally acceptable and does not violate safety plan constraints. Risk: Autonomous operation may conflict with worksite safety policies; recommend explicit safety review.

ASSUMPTION: Availability target of 0.999 (99.9% uptime) is achievable with commercial-grade redundancy and monitoring. Risk: Achieving 0.9999 (99.99%) or higher may require specialized hardware/protocols not yet specified.
ASSUMPTION: Operator can tolerate brief switchover latency (typically 100–500 ms) during failover. Risk: If latency-critical operations are required, switchover time must be specified and validated.

ASSUMPTION: State reconciliation between remote and local control modes can be completed reliably. Risk: Desynchronization during link loss could lead to conflicting commands; recommend explicit conflict resolution strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to capture representative link failure modes (e.g., weather, congestion, equipment faults). Risk: Short evaluation periods may not expose rare failure modes; recommend minimum evaluation duration.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold (≥ 0.999) throughout the agreed evaluation period, with automatic failover to alternative pathways and local autonomous fallback mode, such that operator can resume remote control within [X] seconds of link restoration without loss of critical state or safety violations. Clarify: (1) number and independence of available communication pathways, (2) acceptable switchover latency and state reconciliation time, (3) scope of autonomous fallback functions and safety constraints, (4) minimum evaluation period and representative failure scenarios, (5) definition of 'link availability' (e.g., does it include latency/jitter thresholds, or only binary connectivity?). These clarifications will refine DP5 and enable rigorous IM specification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for managing real-time communication state, link quality monitoring, failover coordination, and autonomous fallback modes between operator and vehicle during teleoperated missions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for primary and redundant communication pathways; carries link quality metrics and failover signals.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; must be resilient to link degradation via fallback mode.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state and link health status back to operator; prioritized during degraded conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical state transitions (e.g., autonomous fallback activation, link loss) to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation owns the operator-vehicle communication lifecycle, link quality monitoring, and switchover logic. Redundant pathways, failover coordination, and hierarchical fallback are core to teleoperation resilience. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services is necessary but well-defined.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own failover logic and autonomous fallback as safety-critical functions, but would duplicate link quality monitoring and communication pathway management. High diffusion: redundant pathways and real-time link monitoring are communication concerns, not safety-specific.

Weak fit: Vehicle module typically owns onboard autonomy and local fallback execution, but not operator-side communication state or link quality monitoring. Significant diffusion: unified state management and switchover logic span operator-vehicle boundary; placing in Vehicle creates asymmetric ownership.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP5 here avoids architectural specificity and diffuses responsibility across all subsystems. Violates principle of most-specific boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 is fundamentally a teleoperation resilience pattern: it ensures continuous operator-vehicle communication under degraded conditions through redundancy, monitoring, and coordinated fallback. The unified state management and switchover logic are intrinsic to the teleoperation control loop, not generic safety or vehicle autonomy functions. Teleoperation is the natural owner because it spans the operator-vehicle communication boundary and must coordinate all three sub-mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) as a coherent system.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module has independent local fallback logic (e.g., obstacle avoidance), coordination between Teleoperation's hierarchical fallback and Vehicle's autonomous behavior may require additional interface definition (e.g., fallback mode state broadcast via Telemetry).
Risk: Safety Services may need real-time visibility into link quality and failover events for safety certification; ensure SafetyAlert interface carries sufficient detail and latency guarantees.

Risk: Redundant communication pathways may involve hardware-level switching (e.g., dual modems, network interfaces); clarify whether pathway selection logic resides in Teleoperation (software) or Network Link (hardware/driver layer).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the unified state management, link quality monitoring, and switchover logic required to demonstrate this metric. Verification can be conducted through instrumentation of the Teleoperation module to log link state transitions, failover events, and availability windows. Traceability from FR to IM is direct and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of operator-vehicle communication lifecycle. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to DP5's three sub-mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, fallback), all of which are communication-layer concerns. Allocation to Teleoperation avoids diffusion across Safety Services, Vehicle, or top-level containers.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 is a coherent, logically complete solution to the FR. It addresses availability through three orthogonal mechanisms: (1) redundant pathways eliminate single points of failure, (2) real-time monitoring with predictive detection enables proactive intervention before link loss, and (3) hierarchical fallback with autonomous mode ensures graceful degradation. The unified state management and switchover logic bind these mechanisms into a coordinated system. DP5 is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation and measurable against the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability).

diffusion_risks: Risk: Redundant communication pathway selection logic (primary vs. redundant link switching) may span Teleoperation (software decision logic) and Network Link (hardware/driver layer). Clarify ownership boundary: does Teleoperation issue abstract 'switch to redundant path' commands, or does it directly manage dual modem/interface state? Recommend explicit interface contract (e.g., 'PathwayControl' command set) to prevent diffusion of failover logic across layers.

Risk: Hierarchical fallback coordination between Teleoperation's operator-side fallback mode and Vehicle's onboard autonomous behavior requires bidirectional state synchronization. If Vehicle module independently executes obstacle avoidance or mission-critical autonomy during fallback, Teleoperation must receive real-time feedback on Vehicle's autonomous state to avoid conflicting commands. Recommend explicit 'FallbackModeState' broadcast via Telemetry interface with defined latency guarantees (e.g., <100ms).

Risk: Safety Services requires real-time visibility into link quality degradation and failover events for safety certification and incident investigation. SafetyAlert interface currently signals only state transitions (e.g., 'fallback activated'). Recommend extending SafetyAlert to include link quality metrics (e.g., signal strength, latency, packet loss) and failover reason codes to enable Safety Services to correlate teleoperation events with safety-critical decisions.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection (DP5 sub-mechanism 2) may require machine learning or statistical models trained on historical link data. If this logic is embedded in Teleoperation, ensure model training and validation are owned by a single module (e.g., Teleoperation or a dedicated Analytics module) to avoid diffusion of model governance across multiple teams.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the correct owner because: (1) it spans the operator-vehicle communication boundary, (2) it owns the communication state machine and link quality monitoring, (3) unified state management and switchover logic are intrinsic to teleoperation control flow, not generic safety or vehicle autonomy functions. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable: SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services is necessary for safety certification and is well-defined via explicit interface. Recommend documenting the three sub-mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) as internal design patterns within Teleoperation to maintain cohesion and prevent future decomposition into separate modules.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Clarify pathway selection logic ownership. Define whether Teleoperation issues abstract 'switch to redundant path' commands (recommended) or directly manages hardware state. Document the interface contract (e.g., 'PathwayControl' command set with response codes) between Teleoperation and Network Link to prevent diffusion of failover logic.

Revision 2: Extend SafetyAlert interface to include link quality metrics and failover reason codes. Recommend adding fields: {link_quality_snapshot: {signal_strength_dbm, latency_ms, packet_loss_pct}, failover_reason: enum, timestamp_utc}. This enables Safety Services to correlate teleoperation events with safety-critical decisions and supports incident investigation.

Revision 3: Document fallback mode state synchronization between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Define 'FallbackModeState' broadcast via Telemetry interface with: {fallback_active: bool, fallback_mode: enum (autonomous_obstacle_avoidance | hold_position | return_to_base), timestamp_utc, latency_guarantee_ms: 100}. This prevents conflicting commands during autonomous fallback.

Revision 4: Assign ownership of predictive degradation detection model (DP5 sub-mechanism 2). If model training/validation is required, designate a single module (Teleoperation or Analytics) as owner to avoid diffusion of model governance. Document model update frequency, validation criteria, and fallback behavior if model becomes unavailable.

Revision 5: Add acceptance criterion detail for evaluation period. Specify: (a) duration of evaluation period (e.g., 30 days continuous operation), (b) measurement methodology (e.g., instrumentation of Teleoperation module, log aggregation), (c) definition of 'availability' (e.g., operator can issue commands and receive telemetry within latency SLA), (d) handling of planned maintenance windows (excluded from availability calculation). This ensures verifiability of the FR.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended revisions expose critical interface and state synchronization gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Given high safety criticality, these gaps pose risks to verifiability, traceability, and incident investigation. Revising the current level (DP and IM specifications) to incorporate explicit interface contracts, extended SafetyAlert semantics, and fallback state synchronization will strengthen the design and prevent downstream diffusion. Once revisions are incorporated and re-gated, the design will be sufficiently mature to decompose into sub-FRs with confidence.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but underspecifies three critical coordination mechanisms: (1) pathway selection logic ownership and interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link (Revision 1), (2) fallback mode state synchronization between Teleoperation and Vehicle with latency guarantees (Revision 3), and (3) predictive degradation detection model governance and fallback behavior (Revision 4). These are design-level concerns that must be resolved before decomposing into sub-FRs. Additionally, the FR acceptance criterion requires clarification on evaluation period, measurement methodology, and availability definition (Revision 5) to ensure verifiability. Revising DP5 to include explicit sub-mechanism specifications and interface contracts will eliminate ambiguity and reduce implementation risk.

assumptions: Teleoperation module owns unified state management and switchover logic for operator-vehicle communication; this ownership is non-negotiable and will not be diffused across Safety Services, Vehicle, or Network Link.

Redundant communication pathways (primary and secondary links) are provisioned at the Network Link layer; Teleoperation issues abstract 'switch to redundant path' commands rather than directly managing hardware state.

Vehicle module can autonomously execute obstacle avoidance or hold-position behavior during fallback; Teleoperation must receive real-time feedback on Vehicle's autonomous state to avoid conflicting commands.

Safety Services requires visibility into link quality degradation and failover events for safety certification and incident investigation; SafetyAlert interface will be extended to include link quality metrics and failover reason codes.

Predictive degradation detection (DP5 sub-mechanism 2) may require machine learning or statistical models; model training, validation, and governance will be owned by a single module (Teleoperation or dedicated Analytics module).
Evaluation period for availability metric (≥0.999) will be defined as continuous operation over a specified duration (e.g., 30 days) with explicit handling of planned maintenance windows.
open_risks: Pathway selection logic diffusion: If Teleoperation directly manages dual modem/interface state instead of issuing abstract commands, failover logic will diffuse across Teleoperation and Network Link, violating separation of concerns. Mitigation: Define explicit 'PathwayControl' interface contract with command set and response codes.

Fallback mode state synchronization latency: If Vehicle's autonomous behavior during fallback is not synchronized with Teleoperation in real-time (<100ms), operator commands may conflict with autonomous actions, creating safety hazards. Mitigation: Define 'FallbackModeState' broadcast via Telemetry interface with latency guarantees.

Safety Services visibility gap: If SafetyAlert interface does not include link quality metrics and failover reason codes, Safety Services cannot correlate teleoperation events with safety-critical decisions or conduct incident investigation. Mitigation: Extend SafetyAlert to include {link_quality_snapshot, failover_reason, timestamp_utc}.

Predictive model governance: If predictive degradation detection model training and validation are not owned by a single module, model governance will diffuse across teams, creating maintenance and validation risks. Mitigation: Assign explicit ownership and document model update frequency, validation criteria, and fallback behavior.

Availability metric verifiability: If evaluation period, measurement methodology, and availability definition are not explicitly specified, verification of the FR will be ambiguous and non-reproducible. Mitigation: Define evaluation period duration, measurement methodology (instrumentation and log aggregation), availability definition (operator command/telemetry latency SLA), and planned maintenance window handling.
evidence_needed: Interface specification document: 'PathwayControl' command set between Teleoperation and Network Link, including command types (switch_to_primary, switch_to_redundant, query_link_status), response codes, and error handling.

State synchronization specification: 'FallbackModeState' broadcast schema via Telemetry interface, including fallback_active (bool), fallback_mode (enum), timestamp_utc, and latency_guarantee_ms (≤100ms).

Extended SafetyAlert specification: Link quality metrics (signal_strength_dbm, latency_ms, packet_loss_pct) and failover reason codes (e.g., link_loss_detected, latency_threshold_exceeded, packet_loss_threshold_exceeded) added to SafetyAlert interface.

Predictive degradation detection design: Model architecture, training data requirements, validation criteria, model update frequency, and fallback behavior if model becomes unavailable. Ownership assignment (Teleoperation or Analytics module).

Availability metric evaluation plan: Specification of evaluation period duration (e.g., 30 days continuous operation), measurement methodology (instrumentation of Teleoperation module, log aggregation, timestamp synchronization), availability definition (operator can issue commands and receive telemetry within latency SLA), and handling of planned maintenance windows (excluded from availability calculation).

Traceability matrix: Mapping of FR to DP5 sub-mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) to revised interface specifications and acceptance criterion details, demonstrating end-to-end verifiability.
